column5: Ultimate Admiral: Dreadnoughts,Hunt: Showdown 1896,Red Dead Redemption 2,n/a,n/a
column6: Two of my 3 most played games are single player games so it doesn't really matter. But Hunt: Showdown 1896 is a team based game. In order to "win," not only do you have to personally know the environment, weaponry, and other factors, you must also get to know your teammates. What strategies they use, how aggressive they are, what weapons they have, if they want money or to fight other players (a choice that can be made). How they react in stressful situations,, what advice they will give in the pursuit of victory. All of this made in the push and pull relatively slow gameplay of Hunt: Showdown. And this is in a small team or 2 or 3 people total. Putting hundreds of hours into this game has helped tune my eye and behavior in team scenarios.

column5: Card Games (Magic\, Balatro\, Slay the Spire),League of Legends (most played by a mile),Counter Strike (not so much now),Satisfactory,Elden Ring (favorite game\, genre\, and studio)
column6:
Absolutely. League of Legends will test and break the most resilient minds know to humankind (any surface level research of the game will instantly prove this notion, playing only 3 or 4 games will also suffice). I have learned over thousands of hours on my solo/duo-ranked-ladder-climbs that to be successful in a team you must find balance between placating aggressive teammates and making decisions that may upset them, but are ultimately correct. Sometimes making a decision or following a call may be an obvious incorrect play, but the emotional boost you give your teammates by backing them up can be worth the sacrifice. People in this game are known for their hostility, and as such by not backing them up on a bad call you may cause them to "lose their cool" and this can cause your team to lose the entire game on the spot. I also play with a duo (League is a 5v5 game, in ranked matchmaking only up to two players can queue for a game together) who is, and don't tell him I said this, extremely temperamental and outburst-prone. He is extremely good at the "micro" (micro-mechanics) of the game (legitimately in the top 1% of players) and I am much more suited for the "macro" (macro-mechanics) of the game (usually floating in the top 4%). We divide our efforts by our strengths, I focus on the fundamentals of the game, tempo, objectives, assisting teammates, and my friend focuses on getting himself as much resources as possible to close out the game.

When I was still a teen I played tons more team based competitive games than I have time for nowadays, all of which helped me grow as a team player, culminating into the still-developing understanding I have now of teamwork.

Games have taught me that to be an effective teammate, and even broader, to be an effective team, you must work toward balance. Micro:Macro, Emotion:Logic, Communication:Action.

Also I want to mention that the Yakuza series of games by "Ryu Ga Gotoku" (Èæç„ÅåÂ¶Ç„Åè) studio has taught me so much about doing what is right in the world and never faltering in helping those around you that I feel compelled to mention it at any opportunity I talk about games in this abstracted manner. Even higher than an individual level, I think the world would only benefit from more people experiencing the series. Each game I play from the series feels like it improves upon my humanity further (they even compelled me to learn a new language). This improves me as a team player because these games, though single-player games, have taught me life lessons about self-reflection, not making the same mistakes of the past, and always striving to improve the world around you. The Yakuza games have taught me that you won't always make the right call, and that sometimes there is no "right call", but regardless of that you must press onward, unwavering in your dedication to do what is right.

Sorry to whoever has to read all this... games are just a major aspect of who I am today and how I got here, the community I have cultivated via games and Discord has rewarded me with lifelong online and IRL friendships.

column6: Gaming definitely plays a role in developing teamwork. It has taught me the importance of sharing resources and adapting your play style to the skills of others. There is always an important role for each player no matter the skill level. I have also learned a lot about teamwork from my experiences through sports. Like, just how important it is to remain selfless and in control of your emotions. Frustration with a task, team-mate, or an opponent can often expose cracks in the team and cause division. By doing your best to keep an even keel, positive team energy, helping others, and knowing when to ask for help success will follow.

In some of the games I play, there are several tasks that need to get accomplished for all players in the team. For instance, in a survival game like Muck or Minecraft, these could include getting armor, getting food, and building a base. If all players do these separately, their efforts are spread thin and they cannot do any one thing very well. It is usually more efficient to divide and conquer, and share the resources with the team. This is a good principle to abide by in a team in most scenarios.

Growing up in the 90's, as the gaming industry had started to boom with an introduction of 3D graphics and console wars; I grew up playing a variety of video games, and game genres with and against my two younger brothers. I can't think of many games that we played around this time that focused on enhancing teamwork, as opposed to dominating your opponent. However, I do recall the enjoyment we had competing against one another, and figuring out new secrets, techniques and strategies as we continued to improve at these games.¬†

On the question of whether or not gaming makes me a better team player, I would say that it pinpoints the perspective of viewing a team as an individual unit, rather than skewing focus to the individual units of a team. Whereas a team should be viewed as a body as a whole as opposed to the individual parts of a mechanical body through the introduction of allocated roles. Each individual part has a role to play. Whether or not that role is successful attributes to the individual unit and impacts the entirety of the team, good or bad. A mechanical body is used in the sense that the individual parts can be replaced in future iterations, however in the present "game", you must make do with the resources at bay.

Sometimes your dealt a bad hand, the only option is to play the hand dealt and do the best you can in support of the mechanical body. With a shared objective of domination, it is not only in the individual units best interest to contribute to the best of their ability, It reflects entirely on the perspective of the team as a whole, and each other individual within that team. Therefore it in the best interest of all individuals units of a team to share resources, learn and work together in pursuit of the overall objective.
